data.title: Valens Semiconductor: A Rerating That Just Started
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Valens Semiconductor Ltd.'s (VLN) recent move reflects the correction of a data error, not a deterioration in fundamentals. With $93.5 million in cash, no debt, and an enterprise value that still implies a heavily discounted operating business, downside risk is largely balance-sheet limited.

What remains underappreciated is the shift toward higher-margin industrial, machine vision, and medical markets with faster adoption cycles. As execution risk replaces solvency risk, the stock appears to be in the early stages of a broader rerating rather than at the end of one.

The Inventory Narrative Was Factually Wrong

Among the more damaging information being floated at the end of 2025 was that of roughly $82 million of inventory being held by Valens Semiconductor. I do not see how that claim survives even minimal scrutiny. Based on the information disclosed through statements released by the firm, at the end of the third quarter of 2025, it had $93.5 million in cash and short-term deposits, around $11 million of inventory, and no financial debt. Total assets were roughly $136.7 million. An $82 million balance of inventory would have necessitated well over $175 million of total assets. That was simply not the situation.

This was not a nuanced accounting disagreement or a difference of interpretation. It was a mathematical impossibility. The best explanation is likely data contamination or ticker confusion with a similarly named industrial company. On its own, such an error should be irrelevant. But not so for a micro-cap.

Screens don't assess context, but rather point out anomalies. After an “inventory risk” tag appears in the system, it's not necessary for it to be debated before being subjected to pressure. It operates in the background, repressing demand in the margins. While it’s true, I don't think this mistake is the cause of all the underperformance in Valens’ stock in the past. Automotive industry weakness, the stigma from being a SPAC, and a lack of sponsorships certainly played a part.

The Balance Sheet Changes the Entire Risk Profile

However, I believe what the market underweights in the stock is the balance sheet. At the end of the third quarter in 2025, the company held cash and short-term bank deposits of $93.5 million; there was no debt, and it had an adjusted EBITDA loss of roughly $4-$5 million. However, it implies multiple years of runway without improvement.

At current market prices, this was approximately 60% of the market cap. So the implied EV of this operating business was approximately $70 million. This is important because what investors are underwriting is not solvency risk. They are underwriting execution risk. It’s a big difference.

A cash-deprived company must prove its case quickly or resort to dilutive financings. Valens does not face such pressure. Valens has enough time for management to find out how demand formation plays out, truly learn about design cycles, and see how the margins work out. It does not face pressure regarding that. The bear case acknowledges the strong balance sheet but does not appreciate the value of time.

In my view, that is a mistake. Time is not a passive asset; it actively reduces risk by allowing the business to mature without sacrificing long-term value to solve short-term financing problems.

The Business is no Longer Focused on Automotive

Valens is still considered a supplier of automotive semiconductors. This view becomes increasingly obsolete. In the third quarter of 2025, about 75% of revenues came from the cross-industry sector of the company, including professional audio-visual, industrial, and medical markets. Automotive only generated about 25% of revenues.

The margin profile supports this trend. In the same period, the cross-industry gross margin was around 69%, while the gross margin for the automotive business was around 43%. Industry gross margins are around 63%. The economic center of the company has shifted out of the auto business. Automotive is strategic, particularly through the MIPI A-PHY, but is not the source of the near-term profit drivers.

This is important because the models of valuation that are based on the cyclical nature of the auto industry tend to undervalue the business as it is already today.

Industrial Machine Vision Is Being Underestimated

Industrial machine vision has a somewhat fuzzy boundary. The company has in-house information that contradicts this. Valens puts the addressable market size for industrial machine vision connectivity at about $460 million by 2028-2029. This is equivalent to 32 million chips a year in 16 million devices."

The fundamental challenge portrayed within this market is not compute. It has high-reach connectivity from distributed sensors to central processing. This happens to be where Valens’ strength comes into effect. In my estimation, machine vision industrial products are among the most underrated sources of future GRMS change since they offer not only a shorter adoption curve but are more like cross-industry products relative to automotive.

Still Cheap After the Rerating

At around $2.48, Valens Semiconductor is no longer a distressed stock, but rather one that is still valued as if the company has little operating leverage and slow productivity gains in the profit margin. Looking at forward valuations, the stock looks inexpensive in comparison to the industry and its own stock history.

Its forward EV/S of around 1.1x and P/S of around 2.3x are well below the semis universe, in which the industry median is closer to 3.5-4.0x. That discount persists even as Valens sustains structurally high gross margins, which were ~63% in Q3 and are guided to moderate to 58–60% in Q4 due primarily to near-term product mix and ramp timing.

The consensus estimates of revenue are showing the company growing from $69.5 million in 2025 to around $100.8 million in 2027, with even further acceleration thereafter as the cross-industry markets mature. The key point here is that this is achieved with the company continuing to be unprofitable in terms of EPS, which serves to drive the traditional multiple well below the company’s operating leverage. This is the problem of valuation. The implication is that the current stock price is reflecting the company’s ongoing lack of profitability rather than an imminent turning point around the corner.

On a balance sheet basis, the level of support for the value is very strong. The P/B is around 1.5, and even just the cash would make up a significant part of the market cap. This translates into a current analyst price target of $3.75, ranging as high as $5.00, reflecting such asymmetry. In my assessment, upside at this point is less about multiples and much more about revenue mix and margin proof, and both are underappreciated at current levels.

Where the Risk Actually Resides

The risk of execution is still present. Timelines in medical and industrial markets can shift, adoption curves may prove uneven, and near-term gross margins may remain volatile. The gross margin decline in Q4 is a major risk in the short term, as it basically illustrates the transition phase of the revenue streams rather than any signal of vulnerability in terms of pricing power and cost structure. Management explained the decline in Q4 largely due to mix and nascent volume, and not so much due to competitive pressures on pricing.

What I do not see is the risk of extinction. The cash position significantly mitigates risk. The technology platform is diversified. There are clear checkpoints over the next 12-18 months when execution can prove or disprove the thesis.

Takeaway

The takeaway here is that Valens Semiconductor is beyond survival risk. The technology firm has a sound balance sheet and strong gross margins. Also, it is fairly diversified into several markets like industrial, machine vision, medical, and automotive. Now, the risk that the investment community faces is the risk of execution.

data.title: Data Update 1 For 2026: The Push And Pull Of Data
data.description:
In my musings on valuation, I have long described myself as more of a number cruncher than a storyteller, but it is because I love numbers for their own sake, rather than a fondness for abstract mathematics. It is that love for numbers that has led me at the beginning of each year since the 1990s to take publicly available data on individual companies, both from their financial statements and from the markets that they are listed and traded on, and try to make sense of that data for a variety of reasons - to gain perspective, to use in my corporate financial analysis and valuations, and to separate information from disinformation. As my access to data has improved, what started as a handful of datasets in my first data update in 1994 has expanded to cover a much wider array of statistics than I had initially envisioned, and my 2026 data updates are now ready. If you are interested in what they contain, please read on.

The Push and Pull of Data

After a year during which we heard more talk about data and data centers than ever before in history, usually in the context of how AI will change our lives, it is worth considering the draw that data has always had on not just businesses but on individuals, as well as the dangers with the proliferation of data and the trust we put on that data.

In a world where we feel adrift and uncertain, the appeal of data is clear. It gives us a sense of control, even if it is only in passing, and provides us with mechanisms for making decisions in the face of uncertainty.

  1. Signal in the noise: Anyone who has to price/value a stock or assess a project at a firm has to make estimates in the face of contradictions, both in viewpoints and in numbers. The entire point of good data analysis is to find the signals in the noise, allowing for reasoned judgments, albeit with the recognition that you will make mistakes.
  2. Coping mechanism for uncertainty: Investors and businesses, when faced with uncertainty, often respond in unhealthy ways, with denial and paralysis as common responses. Here again, data can help in two ways, first by helping you picture the range of possible outcomes and second by bringing in tools (simulations, data visualizations) for incorporating uncertainty into your decision-making.
  3. Prescription against tunnel vision: It is easy to get bogged down in details when faced with having to make investment decisions and lose perspective. One of the advantages of looking at data differences over time and across firms is that it can help you elevate and regain perspective, separating the stuff that matters a lot from that which matters little.
  4. Shield from disinformation: At the risk of getting backlash, I find that people make up stuff and present it as fact. While it is easy to blame social media, which has provided a megaphone for these fabulists, I read and hear statements in the media, ostensibly from experts, politicians and regulators, that cause me to do double takes since they are not just wrong, but easily provable as wrong with the data.

While data clearly has benefits, as a data user, I do know that it comes with costs and consequences, and it behooves us all to be aware of them.

  1. False precision: It is undeniable that attaching a number to something that worries you, whether it be your health or your finances, can provide a sense of comfort, but there is the danger with treating estimates as facts. In one of my upcoming posts, for instance, I will look at the historical equity risk premium, measured by looking at what stocks have earned, on an annual basis, over treasury bonds for the last century. The estimate that I will provide is 7.03% (the average over the entire period), but that number comes with a standard error of 2.05%, resulting in a range from a little less than 4% (7.03% - 2 × 2.05%) to greater than 11%. This estimation error plays out over and over again in almost every number that we use in corporate finance and valuation, and while there is little that can be done about it, its presence should animate how we use the data.
  2. The Role of Bias: I have long argued that we are all biased, albeit in varying degrees and in different directions, and that bias will find its way into the choices we make. With data, this can play out consciously, where we use data estimates that feed into our biases and avoid estimates that work in the opposite direction, but more dangerously, they can also play out subconsciously, in the choices we make. While it is true that practitioners are more exposed to bias, because their rewards and compensation are often tied to the output of their research, the notion that academics are somehow objective because their work is peer-reviewed is laughable, since their incentive systems create their own biases.
  3. Lazy mean reversion: In a series of posts that I wrote about value investing, at least as practiced by many of its old-time practitioners, I argued that it was built around mean reversion, the assumption that the world (and markets) will revert back to historic norms. Thus, you buy low PBV stocks, assuming (and hoping) that those PBV ratios will revert to market averages, and argue that the market is overpriced because the PE ratio today is much higher than it has been historically. That strategy is attractive to those who use it, because mean reversion works much of the time, but it breaks down when markets go through structural shifts that cause permanent departures from the past.
  4. The data did it: As we put data on a pedestal, treating the numbers that emerge from it as the truth, there is also the danger that some analysts who use it view themselves as purely data engineers. While they make recommendations based upon the data, they also refuse to take ownership for their own prescriptions, arguing that it is the data that is responsible.

As the data that we collect and have access to gets richer and deeper, and the tools that we have to analyze that data become more powerful, there are some who see a utopian world where this data access and analysis leads to better decisions and policy as output. Having watched this data revolution play out in investing and markets, I am not so sure, at least in the investing space. Many analysts now complain that they have too much data, not too little, and struggle with data overload. At the same time, a version of Gresham's law seems to be kicking in, where bad data (or misinformation) often drives out good data, leading to worse decisions and policy choices. My advice, gingerly offered, is that as you access data, it is caveat emptor, and that you should do the following with any data (including my own):

(a) Consider the biases and priors of the data provider.

(b) Not use data that comes from black boxes, where providers refuse to detail how they arrived at numbers.

(c) Crosscheck with alternate data providers, for consistency.

Data Coverage

As I mentioned at the start of this post, I started my data estimation for purely selfish reasons, which is that I needed those estimates for my corporate financial analyses and valuations. While my sharing of the data may seem altruistic, the truth is that there is little that is proprietary or special about my data analysis, and almost anyone with the time and access to data can do the same.

Data Sources

At the risk of stating the obvious, you cannot do data analysis without having access to raw data. In 1993, when I did my first estimates, I subscribed to Value Line and bought their company-specific data, which about 2000 US companies and included a subset of items on financial statements, on a compact disc. I used Value Line's industry categorizations to compute industry averages on a few dozen items, and presented them in a few datasets, which I shared with my students. In 2025, my access to data has widened, especially because my NYU affiliation gives me access to S&P Capital IQ and a Bloomberg terminal, which I supplement with subscriptions (mostly free) to online data. It is worth noting that almost all the data from these providers is in the public domain, either in the form of company filings for disclosure or in government macroeconomic data, and the primary benefit (and it is a big one) is easy access.

As my data access has improved, I have added variables to my datasets, but the data items that I report reflect my corporate finance and valuation needs. The figure below provides a partial listing of some of these variables:

As you can see from browsing this list, much of the data that I report is at the micro level, and the only macro data that I report is on variables that I need in valuation, such as default spreads and equity risk premiums. In computing these variables, I have tried to stay consistent with my own thinking and teaching and transparent about my usage. As an illustration for consistency, I have argued for three decades that lease commitments should be treated as debt and that R&D expenditures are capital, not operating, expenses, and my calculations have always reflected those views, even if they were at odds with the accounting rules. In 2019, the accounting rules caught up with my views on lease debt, and while the numbers that I report on debt ratios and invested capital are now closer to the accounting numbers, I continue to do my own computations of lease debt and report on divergences with accounting estimates. With R&D, I remain at odds with accountants, and I report on the affected numbers (like margins and accounting return) with and without my adjustments. On the transparency front, you can find the details of how I computed each variable at this link, and it is entirely possible that you may not agree with my computation, it is in the open.

There are a few final computational details that are worth emphasizing, and especially so if you plan to use this data in your analyses:

  1. With the micro data, I report on industry values rather than on individual companies for two reasons. The first is that my raw data providers are understandably protective of their company-level data and have a dim view of my entry into that space. The second is that if you want company-level data for an individual company or even a subset, that data is, for the most part, already available in the financial filings of the company. Put simply, you don't need Capital IQ or Bloomberg to get to the annual reports of an individual company.
  2. For global statistics, where companies in different countries are included within each industry, and report their financials in different currencies, I download the data converted into US dollars. Thus, numbers that are in absolute value (like total market capitalization) are in US dollars, but most of the statistics that I report are ratios or fractions, where currency is not an issue, at least for measurement. Thus, the PE ratio that I report would be the same for any company in my sample, whether I compute it in US dollars or Chilean pesos, and the same can be said about accounting ratios (margins, accounting returns).
  3. While computing industry averages may seem like a trivial computational challenge, there are two problems you face in large datasets of diverse companies. The first is that there will be individual companies where the data is missing or not available, as is the case with PE ratios for companies with negative earnings. The second is that the companies within a group can vary in size, with very small and large companies in the mix. Consequently, a simple average will be a flawed measure for an industry statistic, since it weighs the very small and the very large companies equally, and while a size-weighted average may seem like a fix, the companies with missing data will remain a problem. My solution, and you may not like it, it to compute aggregated values of variable, and use these aggregated values to compute the representative statistics. Thus, my estimate of the PE ratio for an industry grouping is obtained by dividing the total market capitalization of all companies in the grouping by the total net income of all companies (including money losers) in the grouping.

Since my data is now global, I also report on these variables not only across all companies globally in each industry group, but for regional sub-groupings:

I will admit that this breakdown may look quirky, but it reflects the history of my data updates. The reason Japan gets its own grouping is because when I started my data grouping two decades ago, it was a much larger part of both the global economy and markets. The emerging markets grouping has become larger and more unwieldy over time, as some of the countries in this group had or have acquired developed market status, and as China and India have grown as economies and markets, I have started reporting statistics for them separately, in addition to including them in the emerging markets grouping. Europe, as a region, has become more dispersed in its risk characteristics, with parts of Southern Europe showing the volatility more typical of emerging markets.

-

Data Universe

In the first part of this post, I noted how bias can skew data analysis, and one of the biggest sources of bias is sampling, where you pick a subset of companies and draw the wrong conclusions about companies. Thus, using only the companies in the S&P 500 or companies that have market capitalizations that exceed a billion in your sample in computing industry averages will yield results that reflect what large companies are doing or are priced at, and not the entire market. To reduce this sampling bias, I include all publicly traded companies that have a market price that exceeds zero in my sample, yielding a total sample size of 48,156 companies in my data universe. Note that there will be some sampling bias still left insofar as unlisted and privately owned businesses are not included, but since disclosure requirements for these businesses are much spottier, it is unlikely that we will have datasets that include these ignored companies in the sample in the near future.

In terms of geography, the companies in my sample span the globe, and I will add to my earlier note on regional breakdowns by looking at the number of firms listed and market capitalizations of companies in each sub-region:

Current data link

As you can see, the United States, with 5994 firms and a total market capitalization of $69.8 trillion, continues to have a dominant share of the global market. While US stocks had a good year, up almost 16.8% in the aggregate, the US share of the global market dipped slightly from 48.7% at the end of 2024 to 46.8% at the end of 2025. The best-performing sub-region in 2025 was China, up almost 32.5% in US dollar terms, and the worst, again in US dollar terms, was India, up only 3.31%. Global equities added $26.3 trillion in market capitalization in 2025, up 21.46% for the year.

While I do report averages by industry group, for 95 industry groupings, these are part of broader sectors, and in the table below, you can see the breakdown of the overall sample by sector:

Current data link

Across all global companies, technology is now the largest share of the market, commanding almost 22% of overall market capitalization, followed by financial services with 17.51% and industrials with 12.76%. There is wide divergence across sectors, in terms of market performance in 2025, with technology delivering the highest (20.73%) and real estate and utilities the lowest. There is clearly much more that can be on both the regional and sector analyses that can enrich this analysis, but that will have to wait until the next posts

Usage

My data is open access and freely available, and it is not my place to tell you how to use it. That said, it behooves me to talk about both the users that this data is directed at, as well as the uses that it is best suited for.

  1. For practitioners, not academic researchers: The data that I report is for practitioners in corporate finance, investing and valuation, rather than academic researchers. Thus, all of the data is on the current data link is data as of the start of January 2026, and can be used in assessments and analysis today. If you are doctoral student or researcher, you will be better served going to the raw data or having access to a full data service, but if you lack that access, and want to download and use my industry averages over time, you can use the archived data that I have, with the caveat being that not all data items have long histories and my raw data sources have changed over time.
  2. Starting point, not ending point: If you do decide to use any of my data, please do recognize that it is the starting point for your analysis, not a magic bullet. Thus, if you are pricing a steel company in Thailand, you can start with the EV/EBITDA multiple that I report for emerging market steel companies, but you should adjust that multiple for the characteristics of the company being analyzed.
  3. Take ownership: If you do use my data, whether it be on equity risk premiums or pricing ratios, please try to understand how I compute these numbers (from my classes or writing) and take ownership of the resulting analysis.

If you use my data, and acknowledge me as a source, I thank you, but you do not need to explicitly ask me for permission. The data is in the public domain to be used, not for show, and I am glad that you were able to find a use for it.

The Damodaran Bot!

In 2024, I talked about the Damodaran Bot, an AI entity that had read or watched everything that I have put online (classes, books, writing, spreadsheets) and talked about what I could do to stay ahead of its reach. I argued that AI bots will not only match, but be better than I am, at mechanical and rule-based tasks and that my best pathway to creating a differential advantage was in finding aspects of my work that required multi-disciplinary (numbers plus narrative) and generalist thinking, with intuition and imagination playing a key role. As I looked at the process that I went through to put my datasets together, I realized that there was no aspect of it that a bot cannot do better and faster than I can, and I plan to work on involving my bot more in my data update next year, with the end game of having it take over almost the entire process.

I do think that there is a message here for businesses that are built around collecting and processing data, and charging high prices for that service. Unless they can find other differentials, they are exposed to disruption, with AI doing much of what they do. More generally, to the extent that a great deal of quant investing has been built around smart numbers of people working with large datasets to eke out excess returns, it will become more challenging, not less so, with AI in the mix.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Toll Brothers: There's Clear Recovery Potential (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The housing market hasn’t been easy on Toll Brothers, Inc. (TOL). Weak affordability has led to a prolonged period of lower housing sales, also affecting Toll Brothers’ high price point as the company’s backlog has fallen. The Trump administration is looking at ways to aid housing demand, but definitive measures haven’t been announced yet.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my initial November 2024 article, titled “Toll Brothers: I'm Seeing Positive Signals Ahead.” Continued housing market weakness has led the stock to lose -3% of its value since the article was published, underperforming the S&P 500’s 16% gain.

Housing Market Affordability At Question

The housing market is at an interesting point in time. Affordability issues have continued to weaken housing demand; high mortgage rates and a long-term surge in housing prices have made owning a house more unaffordable, characterized by NAR’s affordability index. The index has stayed low from 2022 forward - although affordability has shown some improvement in late 2025 from slightly moderating mortgage rates and higher selling incentives by homebuilders that have lowered prices, the index remains very weak. Similarly weak affordability was previously seen just before the 2008 financial crisis began.

Trump’s administration seems to be taking the issue seriously. After previously flashing the idea of a 50-year mortgage, the administration recently met with homebuilding executives in early 2026 to address high housing costs. The talks reportedly centered on potential incentives that the administration could offer to incentivize homebuilders to construct more housing.

Measures to aid the housing market are still unsure, and different policies could affect homebuilders differently. Tax incentives, decreasing closing costs, or longer mortgages could help to build housing demand and improve housing demand without cutting into margins, whereas banning institutional buyers could lower housing prices and, in turn, cut into homebuilders’ margins. Reforming zoning codes could help homebuilders build more, indirectly lowering prices and aiding overall sales. The administration’s chosen measures aren’t clear yet.

The homebuilding sector showed significant stock market gains following the news of Commerce Secretary Lutnick’s meeting with executives—the market anticipates measures that ultimately help homebuilders’ bottom line. The S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB) rose by over 5% on Friday. Toll Brothers showed a 7% gain, and other major homebuilders like PulteGroup, Inc. (PHM), Lennar Corporation (LEN), and D.R. Horton, Inc. (DHI) followed with similar gains. The development seems to be clearly positive for homebuilders.

Toll Brothers’ Outlook Is Mixed, But I’m Optimistic for the Long Term

The current housing market backdrop has weighed on Toll Brothers, but the company has still been relatively resilient considering the industry environment. The latest fiscal Q4 report showed stable home closings and even slightly positive 2.7% revenue growth, while weaker pricing power did ultimately lead the company to show a moderate 50 basis point decline in the home sales gross margin. For the entirety of FY2025, Toll Brothers’ net income declined by -14.3% to $1.35 billion. Profitability has stayed great with a 16.9% return on equity, well above my 10.5% cost of equity estimate for the company.

The earnings decline has been more modest than many peers’—Toll Brothers' higher price point has even made the company more resilient in the current macroeconomic landscape than most other homebuilders. The stock market’s good performance has aided affluent homebuyers, and affordability hasn’t been as big of an issue compared to homebuilders with lower average selling prices. Toll Brothers guides an average selling price of $970-990 thousand for FY2026, over double the current median single-family home price of $414 thousand. D.R. Horton, Lennar, and PulteGroup have all shown much more noticeable earnings slowdowns in the past couple of years than Toll Brothers.

The short-term future outlook isn’t quite as resilient, though; home sales revenues are guided to decline by -5.1% in FY2026 at the midpoint, and the adjusted home sales gross margin is guided to decline by an additional 130 basis points to 26% in the fiscal year. Toll Brothers enters the year with just 4647 homes in the backlog, significantly lower than usual. The first half of FY2026 will likely be very weak.

Over the long term, I believe that Toll Brothers is still positioned to grow well. The company has a good amount of optioned and owned land, at a total of 76.1 thousand lots, enabling the company to grow home construction once housing demand improves. Toll Brothers’ current outlook is ultimately restricted by the housing market’s weakness—if the current administration’s measures to aid the housing market turn out successful, Toll Brothers could well return to good earnings growth after FY2026. I believe that current weak growth isn’t likely to last forever, as a clear structural housing undersupply remains.

TOL’s Valuation Has Good Upside from Housing Demand Recovery Potential

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Toll Brothers. I estimate FY2026 to still be quite weak due to housing market issues, but that growth will pick up afterwards with a strengthening housing market and Toll Brothers’ good land position. I estimate the total FY2025-FY2035 revenue CAGR at 2.7% with 2.5% perpetual growth from FY2030 forward.

I now estimate the EBIT margin to stabilize at 17% once housing demand improves. I assume a 25% tax rate, and that Toll Brothers will generate $150 million less annually in free cash flow than in net income due to real estate investments that support estimated growth.

The estimates put Toll Brothers’ fair value at $189.2, suggesting 30% upside as of writing. The current valuation supports good upside in the investment, in my opinion providing an attractive entry point. Upside is ultimately dependent on a housing market recovery. Regardless, Toll Brothers has also continued to generate very healthy earnings even amid more subdued housing demand, supporting the current stock price at a forward P/E of just 11.3.

The fair value estimate is down slightly from $196.7 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of equity of 10.53%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.18% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.46. With a liquidity premium of 0.25%, the cost of equity stands at 10.53%.

Takeaway

Toll Brothers’ outlook is mixed as housing demand is still subdued amid weak affordability. The Trump administration is now looking at ways to boost the housing market, sending enthusiasm around the sector, as improved housing demand could help homebuilders. TOL stock is priced reasonably even with the current earnings outlook, and as a housing market recovery adds meaningful upside potential, I upgrade my rating to Buy.

data.title: Centrus Energy: A Tactical Buy After Meta's Atomic Deal
data.description:
The Real Question

After the news about the "Meta deal" with nuclear companies, my first reaction is to determine if this represents a game-changer or not. In my view, however, the real question is whether we're reacting to the narrative behind the news, or are we finally forced to price in bottlenecks that the market has ignored for years? This is a small difference, but it makes all the difference in the world. Therefore, instead of focusing on how much energy AI will need, we should be focused on who owns and controls the bottlenecks at which energy can be produced to be available continuously and is politically secure. It is at this precise point that Centrus Energy (LEU) comes into play, and my buy rating on this company is tactical. Certainly, it is no bargain in terms of valuation (we see this in the section dedicated); however, the market is starting to account for something that does not appear in the numbers as of yet, nor do I believe it will continue to hide from us for too much longer. So, I repeat, I am buying Centrus Energy, but with a tactical and careful approach.

Why Nuclear is So Important in Today’s Energy Market

I started talking about nuclear more regularly and putting it into my portfolio as early as 2024, not because I knew exactly what was going to happen in advance, but because I knew that once AI took off, semiconductors were not the only ones that would benefit from an AI boom; nuclear would provide a stable form of energy, which would be essential for AI to continue running continuously. AI has transformed energy from being simply a cost component for companies to being a required resource for companies to run their operations. Companies like Google, Facebook, and Amazon (and others) have data centers that must stay operational at all times. They cannot rely on the weather (solar, wind, etc.) or politics for operation. That is why tech companies are investing in energy now, regardless of when the need for that energy may actually occur. This is not a speculative investment, but it is a strategic one.

The markets are starting to see this as well. When Meta made its announcement regarding the future of energy use, the markets reacted quickly, but not all the same. First to rise were the utility and nuclear developer stocks, primarily because they are easier to understand. Next, investors turned their focus toward something else: the supply chain.

When a 20-30-year nuclear development horizon begins to look feasible, the issues are no longer simply whether you can build or maintain the reactors. The issue becomes how you plan to run them - using what fuel and through what supply chain. While there is little discussion of this subject, it is central to the success of nuclear.

Unlike the utility company, Centrus does not respond to individual contracts or incremental megawatts. As energy becomes a constraint in the world, the companies that control the fuel and the supply chain become just as important as the companies that generate electricity. Therefore, the volatility and erratic movements in the Centrus stock price are reflective of a fundamental change in the way the market views nuclear energy.

What Centrus Really Does (and Why It's the Bottleneck)

Centrus is not a utility company producing electrical power. It does not develop or manufacture nuclear reactors. Uranium mining is not part of its activities either. Centrus is located upstream from those activities and performs only one activity: uranium enrichment, the process that converts the raw uranium into fuel suitable for use in nuclear reactors.

The US Dept. of Energy has recently done a study of the nuclear fuel cycle. It must be converted into a form containing a certain percentage of U-235. Conventional reactors use Low-Enriched Uranium (LEU) that contains between 3-5% U-235, while many small modular reactor (SMR) designs, advanced reactors, and even other conventional reactors utilize High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium (HALEU), which contains 10-19.75% U-235. If a reactor does not undergo this conversion process, then it may exist as a conceptual design, but it will never generate electricity.

The important fact about nuclear enrichment capacity is that it is not all the same. In the United States, virtually all commercial enrichment today is performed using gas centrifuges. Gas centrifuges are a difficult-to-implement technology, extremely costly, and heavily regulated. Any new enrichment capacity will take months/years to be implemented licensed and trained personnel to operate.

However, there are additional technologies under development, including laser enrichment, which promises to provide improved efficiency. These technologies are still in the experimental phase and have not been developed to perform at a commercial scale. Therefore, while they present potential for the future, they do not offer a near-term solution.

Centrus provides LEU to operating reactors today, and in the future it will primarily provide HALEU, required for the next generation of reactors. If nuclear power becomes a major player in the support of artificial intelligence (AI) and global energy security, the primary limitation will not be the number of reactors built, but rather if sufficient domestic and reliable certified fuel exists to feed them.

Over the past decade, both the United States and Europe have recognized their over-reliance on foreign companies for this aspect of the nuclear fuel supply chain. This over-reliance was not an ideological decision but an industrial and geopolitical necessity. Therefore, Centrus is becoming viewed as not merely a fuel supplier, but as a strategic asset in the rebuilding of the Western nuclear fuel supply chain.

Expensive for a Reason

First off, let’s take a look at the valuation. First impression: “Centrus is too pricey.” No surprise there; I wouldn’t try to defend those numbers either.

High multiples, overvalued by the market cap relative to fundamentals, and, as always with this stock, very high volatility. If you only looked at those numbers, I think it’s safe to say nobody would ever even consider purchasing this stock.

I’m not making any basic analysis of this stock. What I’m really concerned about is what the market is pricing. It’s apparent the market is willing to pay an extremely high premium for a domestic uranium enrichment supply chain during a time when domestic HALEU production options are so limited, not based on the amount of LEU that Centrus is currently producing or the upcoming near-term earnings.

One area worth paying attention to is cash flow. You'll see from the chart provided that Centrus has shown considerable expansion in its cash flow recently. That may appear to be an indicator of an improvement in the company's profile (on paper, yes), but personally I have a slightly more conservative viewpoint and would interpret that in a different way. The primary reason for the company's strong cash flow generation appears to be the timing of government contracts/work orders and financial arrangements rather than a sustainable stabilization of the company's recurring operating profitability (i.e., structural). It is possible that the company is generating cash at a rate greater than it is spending cash on costs.

This is also the same explanation for why the traditional metrics aren’t effective for this stock. The cash flows from the operation will continue to be erratic, the visibility into future earnings will continue to be based on contracts or government decisions/actions, and the operational leverage of the company will remain very high.

I believe that this trend will continue, and going forward, we will experience strong cash inflows and then more stable cash flows based on the various project execution stages. From a portfolio perspective, I view the recent increase in cash flow as positive, but I do not consider it to be game-changing. Sure, it has improved Centrus' short-term financial position, provides greater flexibility, and reduces near-term dilution risk. However, it still does not make Centrus a company that generates cash consistently. The distinction between these two concepts is important.

I believe this is why Centrus is not just another run-of-the-mill utility. It is also why the company’s stock prices have traded in alternating cycles of extreme optimism, followed by an equally long decline in price. The story here is not that of a “silent compounder,” but rather one of strategic optionality, where the price of the stock has been anticipating future development for a considerable time prior to those changes being incorporated into the financial statements.

So, in this case, high valuation does not automatically mean that you should sell, but it quickly turns into a problem if the story fails to develop. So long as the momentum to strengthen the supply chain continues to appear credible, the market will continue to overlook the short-term numbers. If the momentum begins to slow down or appears to have greater complexity, the multiple would begin to contract very quickly.

The DOE Award

A couple of things about the DOE award need to be clarified. It was nice to see $900 million come from the DOE to Centrus for the purpose of resuming nuclear fuel production at home, pretty much like the arrival of the "cavalry." It is especially good news for existing shareholders (as indicated by the 5.4% spike in the price of Centrus shares as reported by Seeking Alpha).

Mitigating the risk associated with "lack of demand" in the near-term and reinforcing Centrus as a prime provider in the Western supply chain, the explicit public support, increased political visibility and recognition of domestic enrichment as a U.S. national strategic priority were all positives.

For those reading my analyses, I am a very detail-oriented investor and have learned over time to find fault in nearly every investment opportunity in order to avoid investing in the wrong stocks and wasting money. As such, I also look at what this DOE award does not represent.

The $900 million represents a "green light," not the finish line. The overall cost to fully fund all the necessary capital to operate at full enrichment capacity (especially at Piketon) may be much higher than $900 million. While this news is certainly positive, the risk is not that the DOE will pull funding from the project at some future date; rather, the project will continue to be long, fragmented, and subject to many future political decision-making events, task orders, and complementary financing events.

Valuation

I do not believe there is any benefit in attempting to accurately forecast the performance of Centrus, as I see its story as one that will not unfold in a linear fashion and therefore does not fit well within established paradigms. Rather than focusing on making forecasts, the proper method of managing a stock such as Centrus is to consider various potential scenarios to determine which ones may occur and, most importantly, what needs to occur for your thesis to remain intact versus being discredited. That is the manner in which I am able to manage Centrus in my portfolio (which I have tactically positioned, i.e., low weight, high attention) and which, as I previously stated, is not an investment to simply hold onto forever.

Base Scenario

Assuming the Nuclear Energy Industry continues to evolve and that the public continues to support the Nuclear Energy Industry, the HALEU Projects will continue their journey, albeit at a long and unpredictable pace, and therefore, will be difficult to describe to investors. Therefore, if this is the case, Centrus will be very volatile and will swing between euphoric and sell periods; thus, you will be required to treat this as a TACTICAL POSITION.

Bullish Scenario

Assuming the demand for uranium enrichment increases and there is greater clarity on future uranium contractual obligations, the company's stock will appreciate. I do not ask for much; I believe even slight, tangible signs, such as higher volume or operational improvements and/or renewed government support, can indicate a clearer road ahead.

Negative Scenario

The downside risk is that the Company experiences an operational delay in enrichment or uses diluted equity to fund the growth of its enrichment capacity, or the governmental policy priority shifts away from the nuclear energy industry, which would negatively affect the Company. In this case, the problem with the stock is that the historical development of the industry is simply being delayed while the stock price has already discounted much of this possibility. Therefore, the potential for a rapid and extreme compression in the stock price is high due to the high expectations that are embedded in the stock price.

Risks

  1. Timing & Execution: The time to enrich uranium, build a facility, secure necessary licensing, and complete all associated work can take many years. As such, each day (or week) that passes can cause significant fluctuations in the stock due to investors' perception.
  2. Public Support & Politics: One large portion of this thesis (buy rating) relies on public policy and government support for enrichment. If the government decides to delay or change its priorities with respect to enrichment, the investor community may rapidly adjust their expectations of the company's value.
  3. Stock Valuation & Stock Price Volatility: Since the stock price has already reflected a high level of confidence in the positive case, if the story is delayed (regardless of whether it is ultimately proven correct or incorrect), the compression of multiple could be rapid and could result in a very difficult stock price correction.

How I Manage It

Centrus remains a tactical buy, and in my 100% equity portfolio, I limit my holdings in Centrus to no more than 3%. This limited allocation is a function of both the volatility inherent in the stock price, as well as the fact that there is still a lot of story left to unfold before the stock can reach what I believe will be a more normalized valuation range.

Additionally, I usually also disclose my time horizon, but given the type of investment, I consider it variable; it could be 30 days, 90 days, 2 years, etc. I am primarily looking for execution-related items (i.e., increased enrichment capacity, clarification of the HALEU roadmap, evidence of near-term demand) as opposed to EPS growth when reviewing their next set of quarterly results.

Looking forward to the longer term, if I see increasing visibility regarding volume, timing, and Centrus' role within the western nuclear supply chain, the stock may continue to outperform even at higher valuation multiples.

For you, do you think the market has already priced in Centrus' role in the nuclear space appropriately, or are we simply at the beginning of this phase?

data.title: Tracking Tanker Sanctions And Market Impacts
data.description:
Note: This is the complete and unaltered version of an exclusive report published in December 2025 on Value Investor's Edge. This is being made public due to increasing public interest in strengthening sanction enforcement and the consequences.

Introduction

Sanctions activity has escalated over the past couple of years, affecting the crude tanker and product tanker markets.

Until recently, there hadn't been an easy way to track, in aggregate terms, additions to sanction lists since several authorities like the United States Office of Foreign Assets Control (including Advisories), the United Kingdom Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office, the European Union, and the United Nations Security Council maintain their own lists. Some vessels may be sanctioned by an individual authority or by multiple authorities or under multiple programs.

But on February 18, 2025 (VIE Exclusive), a report was published introducing a series of charts designed to track the development of international sanctions on crude and product tankers.

The goal of this report was to address questions regarding how many vessels are sanctioned, what types, and how these sanctions will impact fleet capacity?

This report serves as an update to that original report as well as the last report published on July 3, 2025 (VIE Exclusive).

Note: While this report follows the same format as the previous reports with updated data, there is new information at the end that should provide more color on this issue.

Overview

Since our last report in July 2025, the number of sanctioned crude and product tankers has increased by 18%, moving from 768 to 906.

Sanctions initially had little impact on the market, with many of the targeted tankers continuing to move cargo, composing what became known as the dark fleet or shadow fleet.

However, more earnest efforts to address the issue are beginning to have an impact while the number of tankers sanctioned by at least one governing body continues to grow.

This combination should continue to have a positive impact on rates for compliant vessels as cargoes shift away from the dark fleet.

Crude Tankers

As of December 1, 2025, out of the 906 tankers sanctioned by authorities, 492 of those are crude tankers, a figure that has grown by 13.1% compared to the total of 435 crude tankers listed in the July 3 report.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

With 2,343 crude tankers comprising the current live fleet, these 492 vessels represent 21% in terms of vessel numbers.

But knowing the capacity that is impacted is perhaps even more important.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Currently the crude tanker fleet consists of 465.85 million DWT, with the capacity sanctioned being 76.5 million DWT, representing 18.57% of the fleet.

The main point in tracking all this is to see how escalating sanctions may impact spot and charter rates for crude tankers.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Stricter enforcement of sanctions on tankers has significantly increased since late 2024 and accelerated in 2025. This intensification is part of a concerted effort by the U.S., UK, and EU to disrupt shadow fleet operations carrying sanctioned oil from Russia and Iran.

Some of the more important developments are cited below, but there have been numerous others that have contributed.

  • Late 2024: A sharp rise in sanctions designations began in October 2024, continuing through the end of the year.
  • January 2025: Enforcement actions saw an unprecedented surge, with 183 vessels and 70 companies blacklisted in that single month alone by the U.S..
  • Since March 2025: The U.S. Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) has targeted regional enablers of sanctioned oil trade, including Malaysian oil traders, Indonesian tug operators, and Singapore-based cargo inspection companies.
  • April 2025: OFAC first issued an advisory to the maritime petroleum shipping community in September 2019 regarding sanctions risks for petroleum shipments involving Iran and Syria. This guidance was reissued and updated on April 16, 2025, to include updated information on shadow fleet activities (including Russia), cautioning the private sector about deceptive shipping practices with the intent to pursue significant penalties against companies violating sanctions.
  • August 2025: Panama, a major flag registry jurisdiction, began removing sanctioned tankers from its registry, part of a growing international effort to tighten enforcement. This prompted other flag registries, such as the Maritime Cook Islands, to promptly delete sanctioned or suspicious vessels from their registry, which creates administrative difficulties for the tankers.
  • September 2025: European nations like Finland and Germany implemented specific measures requiring proof of valid civil liability and oil spill insurance certificates for tankers in their waters to target inadequately insured shadow vessels.
  • October 2025: The United States sanctioned Rosneft and Lukoil on October 22, 2025. The UK had imposed sanctions on both companies earlier, on October 15, 2025. Additionally, the EU adopted its 19th sanctions package, with measures against Rosneft and Lukoil, which also included a port access ban for 557 listed Russian shadow fleet vessels.
  • January 2026: The EU plans to block refined product imports made from Russian crude oil.

While other factors like increases in OPEC output and crude tanker demand outpacing supply can be cited for the recent surge in the Baltic Dirty Tanker Index, stronger sanction enforcement applying to a larger number of vessels is now believed to be playing a greater contributing role in securing employment for the compliant fleet.

If enforcement is maintained or grows stronger, some vessel classes will be affected more than others, which is why we offer the following sanction breakdown.

VLCC

As of December 1, 2025, 143 of the 908 live VLCC tankers are under sanctions, representing 15.75% of total vessels.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

In terms of VLCC capacity, 43.82 million DWT are under sanctions out of the 279.72 million DWT on the water, representing 15.67% of the fleet.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

The percentage of sanctioned VLCCs has more than doubled since December 2024 with more stringent enforcement.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Iran has strong ties to the VLCC dark fleet trade, so it's no coincidence rates began to rise around the time that the U.S. Department of the Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) intensified its efforts against Iran’s petroleum and petrochemical exports by sanctioning over 50 individuals, entities, and vessels that facilitate Iranian oil and liquefied petroleum gas sales and shipments from Iran.

Additionally, under the threat of increased tariffs, Adani Group, which is India’s largest private terminal and port operator, announced a ban on all sanctioned vessels from all its operations on September 10, with an implied focus on Russian oil shipments.

Furthermore, Reliance Industries in India recently halted the use of Russian crude at its export-only refinery in Jamnagar to comply with European sanctions. The company, one of the world’s largest refiners, said it has stopped receiving Russian crude at its SEZ unit from November 20 and will switch fully to non-Russian feedstock for exports from December 1. The refinery exports a large portion of finished product to the EU, suggesting this move is likely in preparation for the EU ban on refined products sourced from Russian crude beginning in 2026.

Developments such as this, along with several others, cannot be dismissed when addressing the meteoric rise in VLCC rates. In fact, they are likely a root cause.

Suezmax

The number of sanctioned Suezmax crude tankers has more than doubled since this time last year. Of the 690 Suezmaxes on the water, 116 have been targeted by sanctions, representing 16.81% of the fleet.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

In capacity terms, 15.57% of the live Suezmax fleet is currently under sanctions.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

A similar pattern to VLCC rates is being observed with a sharp rise to finish off 2025.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Aframax

The crude tanker Aframax class has experienced the greatest increase in sanctioned vessels since January 2024, a 904% increase, with the number now sanctioned representing 33.33% of the total fleet.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

In terms of Aframax capacity, 25.22 million DWT are under sanctions out of the 76.41 million DWT on the water, representing 33.01% of the live fleet.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Aframax rates have not been as acutely impacted, with rates only double that of July's levels, whereas both VLCCs and Suezmaxes are up around 3x.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

While the crude Aframax class has seen minimal deliveries and experienced negative capacity adjustments in recent months, the LR2 fleet has registered massive growth.

Source: Clarksons Shipping Intel

As noted in the most recent [Charts] Demand-Side Update, the "spread between the BDTI Aframax TCE and benchmark LR2 routes has become more pronounced recently, which could induce some vessels to engage in cargo switching."

This appears to be happening with Aframax rates hitting a ceiling while product tanker rates have moved upwards. Lloyd's List reported on November 25 Aframax rates are heavily outperforming LR2 rates, prompting a larger-than-usual migration of coated tankers from clean to dirty trades.

Source: Clarksons Shipping Intel

Since there can be some interchangeability between the Aframax and LR2 classes, the next series of charts examines these classes as a whole before we move on to the product tanker segment.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Product Tankers

Looking at product tankers that are 10k DWT and larger, out of the 3,525 vessels currently on the water, a total of 411 vessels have been sanctioned, representing 11.66% of the fleet.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

In terms of capacity, 20.87 million DWT is sanctioned out of 197.91 million DWT, representing 10.55%.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Though it is still too early to draw conclusions for the month of December, it is noteworthy that the past week has seen the highest reading of the BCTI since the week of June 7, 2024. This move could be attributed to a few factors: stronger enforcement of sanctions, cargo switching, and seasonal strength, which peaks in December.

LR2

Looking specifically at the LR2 class, out of the 504 live vessels, 74 of those have been sanctioned, representing 14.68% of the fleet. This makes LR2s the most impacted class of product tankers, which is significant because their large size comprises a high amount of total fleet capacity.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

In capacity terms, 8.11 million DWT out of 55.86 million DWT are sanctioned, representing 14.52% of the fleet.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Spot rates vary significantly across the numerous trading routes, making 6-month time charter rates the best way to look at how the market is collectively responding over the short term.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

TC rates are slower to adjust but often are the best way to discern longer-term trends. Here we observe that LR2 rates have attempted to put in a bottom, with November showing the highest average all year despite the strong capacity influx for product tankers, which can be mostly attributed to LR2 deliveries.

LR1

There are 383 LR1 product tankers currently trading, with 42 of those under sanctions, representing 10.97% of the fleet.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

In terms of LR1 capacity, 3.04 million DWT is under sanctions out of the 28.21 million DWT on the water, representing 10.78%.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Though there has been a sharp increase in sanctioned LR1s, November 2025 rates were not able to move above November of 2024.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

This could be due to a preference for newer LR2 tonnage among charterers, which benefits from improved economies of scale due to both the size and modernity.

MR1 & MR2

Together, the MR1 and MR2 classes cover product tankers ranging in size from 10,000 DWT to 54,999 DWT and comprise the majority of tonnage on the water.

Out of the 2,615 MR1 and MR2 tankers on the water, 193 have been sanctioned, representing 7.38% of the fleet.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

In capacity terms, 8.15 million DWT out of 109.79 million DWT are sanctioned, representing 7.42% of the fleet.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Together, the MR1 and MR2 classes have the lowest percentage of vessels out of the live fleet targeted by sanctions, which is why it is no surprise that 6-month TC rates have shown only minimal impact, though they are improving from the mid-2025 lows.

Source: Value Investor's Edge

LNG

Source: Value Investor's Edge

LPG

Source: Value Investor's Edge

Additional Commentary

In the July 2025 Sanctions Update, a section entitled "Breaking Point" examined the supply of tankers in the dark fleet compared to suspected cargo volumes.

The conclusion of that section read: "We are likely approaching, or have already reached, the maximum amount of damage the formation of the tanker dark fleet can inflict on the legitimate trade."

That signaled the headwinds experienced by the formation and growth of the dark fleet were waning, with any further market impact likely to be stymied or even nonexistent. Now, with greater enforcement of sanctions, not only have headwinds ceased, but tailwinds have formed as more cargoes are finding their way on compliant vessels. This presents a notable shift in market dynamics from just six months ago.

Also, and perhaps most importantly, older vessels are being removed from the conventional trading fleet at an accelerating pace due to the increasing number of vessels facing sanctions. Remember, not all vessels that engage in the shadow trade are exclusively confined to the shadow trade, which is why sanctions on vessels engaging in both compliant and shadow trades are having an impact on compliant supply.

Since the start of 2025, the capacity being sanctioned has grown at a faster rate than new builds being delivered, which has amplified supply-side constraints for the legitimate trade.

This new market dynamic carries with it the promise of far more favorable conditions for the compliant fleet.

So this leads to the question if dark fleet vessels can ever return to the compliant trade. Indeed, sanctions are not permanent, and their lifting often depends on targeted countries or entities taking satisfactory measures, highlighting the dynamic nature of international trade restrictions and the importance of continuous compliance and vigilance in the maritime sector.

While each sanctioning body has different rules and regulations, the OFAC delisting process involves petitioning OFAC for removal from sanctions lists, which may be granted based on positive behavioral changes, if the sanction's basis no longer exists, or if there was a mistaken identity. Additionally, the potential exists that owners may be forced to sell the vessel, with proceeds going into a blocked account. This, in itself, presents a costly and time-consuming process, and if these sanctioned vessels are found to be continuing with illicit trading during this review, it is likely they will not be delisted.

If a tanker has been sanctioned by more than one governing body, which many have, it will need to apply for delisting with each respective governing body, adding to the time and cost needed to become compliant.

Other challenges exist for many of these dark fleet tankers, most of which are older and have fallen out of IMO compliance. Numerous vessels have shirked special surveys, which are required to ensure seaworthy conditions for the safety of the crew and their surroundings.

Those vessels that have fallen out of IMO compliance may face costly hurdles to regain that compliance due to both the special survey cost and the required repairs due to deferred maintenance. This presents a second costly and time-consuming process.

Some are not likely to ever return since a number of dark fleet tankers would have likely been retired within the past few years (based on economic viability when engaging in the compliant trade) had the formation of the illicit oil/product trade not developed, which provided them with a second life.

Overall, it has been estimated that approximately two-thirds to three-quarters of sanctioned vessels may never again return to the compliant trade due to these hurdles.

However, higher rates for compliant vessels will provide greater incentive for owners to get their vessels delisted.

Another consideration for shadow fleet owners is that while sanctions are growing more intense, owners could lose their ships entirely. This is happening in two ways:

First, the Indonesian government is auctioning off a US-sanctioned supertanker and its cargo of crude oil for at least $70 million. The Arman 114, a VLCC flagged to Iran, was seized for allegedly transferring oil to another tanker and spilling some in the process. While this seizure and sale is an unusual move, it comes as countries and shippers fret over how to handle aging, sanctioned vessels that are part of a growing dark fleet and could serve as a guide for further enforcement, especially if nations can pocket millions of dollars in the process with the blessing of Western nations.

Second, on November 28, Ukraine’s security service claimed strikes on two ocean-going tankers sanctioned for carrying Russian oil, which were hit by blasts off Turkey’s Black Sea coast. Both tankers sustained critical damage, with the Kairos catching fire, leading to the evacuation of its 25 crew members and disabling the vessel, while the Virat was struck near the engine room but remained in stable condition.

This action was likely taken to hinder Russian revenues but also to force dark fleet owners (who can be difficult to pinpoint due to shell companies) to more closely examine the new risk/reward dynamic that would come along with Ukraine targeting vessels.

The Defense Intelligence of Ukraine stated, "The shadow tanker fleet continues to provide multibillion-dollar revenues for the Kremlin, bypassing sanctions, disguising its activities under the flags of third countries, and using complex schemes to conceal owners."

However, these attacks raise concerns since they could injure non-combatants on non-military vessels whose operation is safeguarded by norms of international law (sanctioned or not).

Finally, are sanctions working?

An analysis by Kpler indicates that the impact of sanctions on tanker productivity and employment varies significantly depending on the sanctioning body. Comparing a vessel’s productivity as described by ton-miles before and after inclusion on sanctions lists demonstrates that OFAC sanctions are by far the most effective. Vessels added to OFAC’s lists experience an average productivity decline of 70%. In comparison, vessels sanctioned exclusively by the EU or UK see a much smaller reduction, estimated at 30%. This suggests that Russia’s trading partners are more hesitant to challenge US institutions, possibly reflecting significant exposure to the US dollar for trade and financing.

Drop in Vessel’s Ton-miles (T/ms) Post-sanctions

Source: Kpler

Finally, Russian crude volumes have recently hit a new multi-year low, likely the result of Trump’s 23 October sanctions against Russia’s two largest oil companies, Rosneft and Lukoil, which took full effect on 21 November.

Source: Veson Nautical

Even more telling is that, according to JPMorgan, around 1.4m barrels per day of Russian oil, nearly a third of the country’s seaborne export capacity, is currently sitting on tankers as discharge operations in importing nations have slowed considerably.

The data underscores how US and European restrictions are increasingly reshaping global crude logistics.

Conclusion

The number of sanctioned vessels continues to climb, with 18.57% of total crude tanker capacity now sanctioned by at least one governing body.

For VLCCs, Suezmaxes, and Aframaxes, that total comes to 15.67%, 15.57%, and 33.01%, respectively.

Since the LR2s have the ability to switch between dirty and clean trades, it was noted that 25.20% of capacity for the combined Aframax and LR2 fleets is currently under sanctions.

10.55% of product tanker capacity is currently sanctioned, with the LR2, LR1, and MR classes at 14.52%, 10.78%, and 7.42%, respectively.

Starting in 2025, Western governments have intensified enforcement measures targeting sanctioned vessels as well as entities with which they do business. This is evidenced by large-scale actions taken by the United States OFAC as well as the EU and UK.

US-based sanctions are significantly more effective at stopping individual vessels from operating than EU or UK sanctions alone, likely due to the threat of secondary sanctions and the power of the US dollar, with OFAC-sanctioned vessels experiencing an average productivity decline of 70% compared to a 30% decline for the EU/UK standalone sanctions.

While the goal of completely cutting off illicit crude oil exports has not been met, recent large-scale and coordinated enforcement actions have undeniably increased the cost and complexity of the dark fleet's seaborne oil trade.

This has led to significant benefits for compliant tankers, as the sharp drop in available shadow tanker capacity has fostered greater utilization of the legitimate fleet, leading to increasing spot and even time charter rates.

Many sanctioned tankers may find it difficult to return to compliant trading. With Russian/Ukraine peace talks ongoing, a ceasefire could mean a lifting of crude oil sanctions on Russia. If Russian volumes return to the global stage, from out of the shadows, this could provide yet another tailwind for crude and product tanker rates if those volumes are carried on compliant vessels instead of the shadow fleet, which is anticipated.

data.title: Damage To Monetary Credibility May Now Be Irreversible: Stay In Cash At Your Own Risk
data.description:
Just a few years ago, it may have sounded highly unlikely that a respected NATO member could:

  1. Pressure their Central Bank to cut rates, using the judiciary system to try to bring monetary policy under full control of the government.

  2. Run a consistent and uncontrolled budget deficit for a number of years to finance ballooning public spending and ever-increasing interest payments.

  3. Use their strength as a key NATO member to pursue military operations without the support of international law or supranational institutions.

I am, of course, talking about the country of Türkiye.

Under its current government, Türkiye has pursued a full politicization of its central bank, run a consistent deficit, and shut down Russian military assets. As a result, the country experienced consistent 50%+ inflation rates and saw the Turkish Lira (TRY:USD) devalue by more than 99% against the USD.

Readers may easily see the parallel I am trying to make with America today. I think the Fed chair's recently released video represents a turning point in the credibility of U.S. monetary policy.

Jerome Powell is the first Fed chair to explicitly call out pressures from a sitting administration in regards of monetary policy:

This unprecedented action should be seen in the broader context of the administration’s threats and ongoing pressures. [...] This new threat is [...] a consequence of the Federal Reserve’s setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President. This is about [...] whether [...] monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation.

If there were any doubts about whether the current U.S. administration is trying to force the hand of the Fed, Powell’s statement cancels any doubt. I think this may act as a catalyst for equity and bond markets to start repricing U.S. assets.

America is no Türkiye, but the cat is out of the bag

I see America as a far more stable political system than that of Türkiye. The U.S. economic fabric is also unparalleled in the world, and the rule of law (including an independent judiciary system) is still a thing in America. In fact, I do not expect anything of the magnitude of what happened to Türkiye to happen in the U.S. (more of my thoughts on market implications later in the article).

However, I think Powell’s statement is a “cat out of the bag” moment, which may awaken markets to the fact that American governments may get future access to both fiscal and monetary tools going forward.

In this sense, it will be very difficult for any future administration to return credibility to the system. First, because today’s pressure on the Fed only adds to a secular trend of erosion in America’s financial credibility. Borderline chronic budget deficits, ballooning public debt, and ever-expanding money supply are all forms of currency debasement that started with the U.S. abandonment of the gold standard in 1971.

More importantly, if the Fed is eventually brought under quasi-direct government control, it will be a politically costly maneuver for any future President to reverse this. Returning complete independence to the Fed would inevitably involve hiking rates and hurting the economy, a tough sell to voters. Additionally, it will be very convenient for any future administration to have a “softer” and less independent Fed.

Finally, even if an eventual “politicization” of the Fed is reversed, investors will know that this can be undone again. In other terms, the markets may simply not care about politics and start pricing in a new reality where US administrations have almost direct power over both fiscal and monetary tools. This may already start happening in the next few weeks.

What happens next? The S&P 500 may finally exit its bear market (measured in gold)

As I mentioned above, I think today’s statement from the Fed’s chair may act as a catalyst for markets to awaken to a new reality where America can no longer be trusted. In this regard, I do expect what happens next to resonate with what happened in Türkiye, even to a far different scale:

  • The bond market may start decoupling from the Fed’s policies and guidance. It may also start pricing in a different inflation rate than that of official data. As a result, US bonds may start demanding a significant premium. This would be bearish for funds such as the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF Shares (BND) and the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG), as new bonds could demand higher premiums than those in the funds, regardless of where the Fed’s rates stand.

  • The bond market may start demanding higher yields to finance a less financially and monetarily credible America. The United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) may see a spike, as markets discount structurally higher inflation.

  • I also think the equity markets and the S&P 500 Index (SP500) could keep seeing new ATHs and keep growing, at least nominally. That’s the effect of capital moving away from USD cash (eroded by an inflation rate that no institution stands to seriously fight anymore) into assets generating a future cash flow.

  • Assets such as the SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (GLD) and Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) will also see ever-higher nominal valuations, in virtue of their scarcity and independence from governmental intervention and currency debasement. I think they can be used by investors as useful proxies to measure returns.

The clear “loser” in this scenario is the US Dollar. The USD cannot be considered a safe haven anymore, and in my view, it can hardly have a role in a portfolio anymore. In a world where the government has both fiscal and monetary tools available to use, staying invested in assets of any kind is a necessity.

In this scenario, I think we may finally see the S&P 500 starting to grow again when measured in gold. In terms of gold, the S&P 500 is currently in a deep bear market, down ~40% from its most recent All-Time-High in 2021 and a whopping ~70% from its All-Time-High of 2001 (see chart below).

Why is that? My idea is that America’s credibility throughout the last century is what kept gold artificially “depressed” in USD terms. So depressed, in fact, that today’s S&P 500’s valuation in gold terms is roughly at the levels of the 1960s.

The paradigm is now shifting. With markets awakening to a new reality, they may finally start to re-price equities in gold terms. Ultimately, this translates into a choice:

  1. Owning US stocks, i.e., the right to future earnings from the most prominent and innovative companies on Earth.

  2. Owning an asset just in virtue of its scarcity.

I think the right to future earnings is a far better asset to hold than a shiny rock that can be mined out of the ground. It is unsurprising, of course, in the current context, that said shiny rock is performing so well. But long term, I believe markets will start putting a higher value on yield-generating assets such as stocks rather than gold.

That’s why I, personally, have most of my portfolio invested in equities, and I keep rating the S&P 500 a Buy, even if I am also exposed to gold and Bitcoin. Rather than owning (predominantly) gold, I believe investors would be better off using gold as a unit of measurement for their returns.

Risks to my thesis

Any type of policymaking is impossible to predict by definition. It is entirely possible that the current administration may reduce its pressure on the Fed, following Powell’s statement. Ultimately, fiscal and monetary rigor can always return out of sheer political will. While I, personally, deem that very unlikely, this is the fundamental risk of my thesis.

Should we see future U.S. administrations running tight budgets and give new credibility to the U.S. dollar, we could see a “great reset” in equity valuations expressed in USD terms. Such a reset, in my view, would also concern gold and any other asset. A trustworthy currency can be considered a safe haven in itself, drawing capital away from assets whose volatility and value are difficult to grasp.

Conclusion: stop thinking in USD, start thinking in gold
